title: Using Dictate in Microsoft Word
subtitle: Student Guide
purpose: Help students use Microsoft Word Dictate to speak their ideas into a Word document.

introduction.heading: Introduction to Microsoft Word Dictate
introduction.whatIsIt: Dictate is a feature in Microsoft Word that lets you speak your ideas out loud and have them typed into your document.
introduction.whyUseIt: Dictate can help you get ideas down quickly, reduce typing fatigue, and focus on what you want to say before editing your work.
introduction.imagePlaceholder: Microsoft Word Home tab with the Dictate button highlighted
introduction.imageDescription: Microsoft Word open on a blank document with the Dictate microphone button highlighted on the Home tab.

whatYouNeed: A device with Microsoft Word installed
A working microphone, either built-in or external
Internet access, if required by your version of Microsoft Word
gettingStarted.beforeYouBegin: Check that your microphone is connected and working. Close other apps that may be using your microphone.
gettingStarted.tip: Using headphones with a microphone can improve accuracy and reduce background noise.

steps.title: Open Microsoft Word
instructions: Open Microsoft Word on your device.
Wait for the start screen or your recent documents to appear.
steps.tip: Open Word before you start speaking so you can check that Dictate is available.
steps.imageDescription: Microsoft Word start screen showing the option to create a blank document.

steps.title: Open a blank document
instructions: Select Blank document from the start screen.
Click into the document where you want your text to appear.
steps.imageDescription: A blank Microsoft Word document with the cursor placed on the page.

steps.title: Select Dictate from the Home tab
instructions: Select Dictate, shown as a microphone icon.
steps.imagePlaceholder: Dictate button on the Home tab
steps.imageDescription: Microsoft Word Home tab with the Dictate microphone button highlighted.

steps.title: Allow microphone access
instructions: If prompted, allow Microsoft Word to access your microphone.
Check that the Dictate microphone is switched on.
steps.tip: If you accidentally block microphone access, check your device privacy or microphone settings.
steps.imageDescription: A microphone permission prompt asking whether Microsoft Word can access the microphone.

steps.title: Start speaking clearly
instructions: Watch as your words appear as text in the document.
steps.imageDescription: A Word document showing spoken words appearing as typed text while Dictate is active.

steps.title: Say punctuation aloud
instructions: Say punctuation such as comma, full stop or new paragraph when needed.
Use short sections so the text is easier to review.
steps.tip: You can edit the wording after dictating, so focus first on getting your ideas onto the page.
steps.imageDescription: A Word document showing a dictated paragraph with punctuation added.

steps.title: Stop Dictate
instructions: Select the Dictate button again to stop recording.
Check that the microphone icon is no longer active.
steps.imageDescription: The Microsoft Word Dictate button shown in its inactive state.

steps.title: Review and save your document
instructions: Correct any words, punctuation or spacing that need editing.
Select File, then Save As, and choose where to save the document.
steps.tip: Dictate is useful for drafting, but it is still important to review your work before submitting or sharing it.
steps.imagePlaceholder: Save As screen in Microsoft Word
steps.imageDescription: Microsoft Word Save As screen showing options for saving the document.

helpfulTips.whyItHelps: Helps Dictate recognise your words more accurately.
Makes your ideas easier to separate and review.
Reduces background noise and improves accuracy.
Helps you catch any words or punctuation that Dictate has misheard.

troubleshooting.whatToTry: Check that Microsoft Word is open, you are signed in if required, and your internet connection is working.
Check microphone access and make sure the Dictate microphone is switched on.
Speak more slowly, reduce background noise, and review the text after dictating.
Turn Dictate off and on again. If the issue continues, restart Word or check your microphone settings.

faqs.question: Do I need to type at all?
faqs.answer: You can use Dictate for much of your drafting, but you should still review and edit your work.

faqs.question: Can I use Dictate for assignments?
faqs.answer: Yes. Dictate can be useful for drafting ideas, notes and assignment paragraphs.

faqs.question: Does Dictate work with different accents?
faqs.answer: Yes, although accuracy can vary. Speaking clearly and reviewing your text can help.

faqs.question: Can I use Dictate for long documents?
faqs.answer: Yes, but dictating in shorter sections is usually easier to manage and review.

whereToGetHelp: [Insert confirmed accessibility or assistive technology support contact]
